0: <title>Comparing the environmental impacts of pasture-based and confinement-based dairy systems in Nova Scotia (Canada) using life cycle assessment </title>
Nicole Arsenault¹, Peter Tyedmers1* and Alan Fredeen2

1 School for Resource and Environmental Studies, Dalhousie University, Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada; and 2 Department of Plant and Animal Sciences, Nova Scotia Agricultural College, Truro, Nova Scotia, Canada

A life cycle assessment (LCA) of dairy systems in Nova Scotia was conducted to compare environmental impacts of typical pasture and confinement operations. Data on material and energy inputs and outputs of these systems were obtained from local researchers and industry, and life cycle impacts in 11 categories were quantified. Use of concentrate feeds, N fertilizers, transport fuels and electricity were dominant contributors to environmental impacts. Somewhat surprisingly, grazing COWS for five months per year (typical of pasture systems in Nova Scotia) had little effect on overall environmental impact. Scenario modelling suggests, however, that prolonged grazing is potentially beneficial. Compared with total confinement, a seven-month grazing scenario performed better in seven of the environmental impact categories evaluated with greatest potential improvements associated with acidification potential, ozone depletion potential, human toxicity and fresh water ecotoxicity. In contrast, land use was the only category in which an increased reliance on pasture is predicted to result in a marked increase in environmental impact.
Keywords: confinement-based system, dairy systems, environmental impact, life cycle assessment, pasture-based dairy system

2: Milk, considered a staple food by many, is being produced with increasing intensity worldwide. Environmental impacts commonly associated with dairy systems include greenhouse gas emissions (CH4 from enteric fermentation and manure storage, N2O from manure storage) (Boadi et al., 2004; Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996; Kirchgessner et al., 1991; Kulling et al., 2001; Olsen et al., 2003; Steed & Hashimoto,

1994; Williams, 1993), eutrophication and acidifi- cation as a result of nutrient losses (Dou et al., 1996; Hutson et al., 1998; Stout et al., 1997; 1998) and land appropriation (Baessler & Klotz, 2006; Belfrage et al., 2005; Hodgson et al., 2005; Robinson & Sutherland, 2002).

It is commonly argued that industrial-type, confinement-based dairy systems make more effi- cient use of resources than do traditional, pasture- based systems. The shift from pasture to confine- ment systems is driven, in part, by the potential to control diets and increase milk yield (Versteeg, 2001). Others, however, feel that the gains in pro- duction do not balance the increased inputs of nutri- ents and energy, and associated emissions, that are

characteristic of intensification (Bleken et al., 2005; Fredeen et al., 2002; Main et al., 2001), and research has shown that the use of pasture can increase the profitability and resilience of dairy farms (Fredeen et al., 2002; Hanson et al., 1998; Main et al., 2001). In the face of these conflicting arguments, many scientists are recommending a systems-based approach for evaluating the sustainability of agri- cultural systems (Ikerd, 1993; Okey, 1996; Stockle et al., 1994). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a detailed systems analysis tool, which has experi- enced a great deal of growth and development since the early 1990s (Baumann & Tillman, 2004). Though this tool has been used historically to assess the potential environmental impacts of indus- trial systems, LCA has recently been used to assess the environmental impacts associated with a wide range of agricultural systems. The environmental impacts of dairy systems (Berlin, 2002; Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Hospido et al., 2003), other livestock systems (Basset-Mens & van der Werf, 2005; Ogino et al., 2004) and veg- etable and fruit production systems (Brentrup, 2003; Mila i Canals et al., 2006) have all been mod- elled using LCA. Despite the increasing global popularity of LCA to assess the impacts of dairy and other livestock systems, as yet no such analyses have been undertaken on North American pro- duction systems (Peters & Estrela, 2003). The objective of this research was to compare the life cycle material and energy flows and associated environmental impacts of dairy systems employing seasonal use of pasture and those using year-round confinement in Nova Scotia (Canada). The specific goals of this study were (1) to perform an inventory of all inputs to, and emissions from, average con- temporary pasture- and confinement-based dairy farm systems in Nova Scotia; (2) to quantify the potential environmental impacts of these systems; (3) to identify potential hotspots of environmental impacts; (4) and to describe how the two systems compare with respect to impacts. While this work does not provide an exhaustive basis upon which the sustainability of dairy pro- duction systems can be evaluated, it does provide a comprehensive assessment of many of the biophysical impacts of typical dairy farm systems in Nova Scotia. It is hoped that this research will be of interest to dairy farmers and industry

professionals, whose decisions directly affect the methods of production in this industry, and there- fore affect the degree to which these activities are environmentally harmful. Furthermore, we hope to stimulate greater interest in using LCA to assess the environmental burdens associated with agricul- tural production in Canada.

5: <header>Dairy farms in Nova Scotia </header>

The Canadian dairy industry operates under a supply management system, the goal of which is to match milk supply to the demand within the country. National and provincial production targets are set, and production is controlled using a quota system. There is almost no export focus in the industry, and with net dairy farm receipts total- ling $4.8 billion (2005), based solely on the dom- estic market, Canada's dairy industry ranks fourth in the agriculture sector. Holsteins have become the dominant dairy cattle breed used in Canada, accounting for 93% of the national herd (AAFC, 2006). There is a national trend in the Canadian dairy industry towards fewer, larger, total confine- ment and higher-producing farms. This trend is consistent across all provinces (AAFC, 2005).

Currently, approximately 35% of dairy farms in Nova Scotia provide some forage from pasture, but this group is shrinking (pers. comm., Mr. M. Main, Nova Scotia Agricultural College (NSAC) and M. DeGraaff, Soil & Crop Improvement Association of Nova Scotia, 2006). Fewer than 5% of dairy farms in Nova Scotia make intensive use of pasture to feed their dairy herds.

In confinement-based dairy systems, COWS are fed a highly-controlled diet of grains and stored forages (mainly in the form of hay silage and corn silage). Concentrate feed is often purchased and trans- ported to the farm, whereas forage crops (hay and silage corn) are typically grown on-farm (pers. comm., Mr. M. Main, NSAC and Daniel Scothorn, AgraPoint International Inc.). These systems tend to handle more COWS than do grazing systems, and are characterized by high milk yields, a pattern consistent with comparable systems internationally (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Hanson et al., 1998).
Pasture-based dairy systems are characterized by the intensive use of a combination of pasture and stored forages during the late spring, summer

and early autumn. In Nova Scotia, COWS graze approximately 150 days per year, though there is potential to extend the grazing season. Large quan- tities of concentrate feeds are also used, especially during the winter, when the COWS are not grazing. Less concentrate feed is required by grazing cows, because well-maintained pasture is a highly nutri- tious food source. These systems tend to handle fewer COWS than do confinement systems, but COWS are still producing comparably high milk yields, with a small decrease in production during the grazing season.

To facilitate comparison, two 'typical' production systems were defined with input from academics and industry extension staff. The basic features of these systems, concentrating on the ways in which they are different, are described in Table 1.

9: <header>Materials and methods </header>
Life cycle assessment
The Society of Environmental Toxicology and Chem- istry (SETAC), the first international organization to
devote itself to the development of life cycle assess- ment (LCA), defines it as follows:

a process to evaluate the environmental burdens associated with a product, process, or activity by identifying and quantifying energy and materials used and wastes released to the environ- ment; to assess the impact of those energy and material uses and releases to the environment; and to identify and evaluate opportunities to affect [sic] environmental improvements. The assessment includes the entire life-cycle of the product, process, or activity, encompassing extracting and processing raw materials; manu- facturing; transportation and distribution; use, re-use, maintenance; recycling, and final disposal. (Consoli et al., 1993)

Life cycle assessment methodology is continuously evolving and being adapted to meet specific goals. The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) has, however, produced a standard (ISO, 2006a, 2006b), which attempts to harmonize formal LCA methodologies. We have endeavoured, in this analysis, to conform to the guidelines set out by ISO, as interpreted and expanded on by Guinée

(2001a). Within these guidelines, we have also tailored our analysis to the unique requirements of an agricultural LCA. Attempts have been made to harmonize agricultural LCA methodologies (Audsley, 1997), but some important issues remain unclear when conducting LCAs in this sector. Examples include the allocation of environmental burdens among agricultural co-products and the incorporation of impacts on biodiversity. While LCA is an iterative process, wherein earlier steps may be revisited repeatedly to permit refinement, it is generally recommended that LCA practitioners follow a four phase approach encompassing: (1) goal and scope definition; (2) life cycle inventory (LCI); (3) life cycle impact assessment (LCIA); and (4) interpretation (Consoli et al., 1993; Guinee, 2001a; ISO, 2006a, 2006b).

11: <header>Scoping and goal definition </header>

In this phase, the system (or systems) to be studied and the functional unit (the reference to which impacts are related) are defined, the intended audi- ence is identified, and the depth and boundaries of the study are determined. Furthermore, methodo- logical decisions regarding data quality limitations and the allocation of environmental impacts among co-products are also made at this stage
(Consoli et al., 1993; Curran, 1996; Guinée, 2001a; ISO, 2006a, 2006b).

The boundaries of the study systems and the life cycle processes included in this analysis are indi- cated in Figure 1. The upstream boundary encom- passes the production of fertilizers and pesticides and the production of fuel and electricity. The downstream boundary is the farm gate. All transport within and between subsystems were accounted for. The transport, processing and packa- ging of milk, however, is not included in this study. In Nova Scotia, all milk is transported and processed in the same way, and an LCA of milk packaging in Nova Scotia has previously been performed (Giroux, 1999). Furthermore, the production of buildings and machinery, medicines and cleaning agents has not been considered, as these are similar for both systems. The temporal boundary for this study is one year.

The functional unit for this study is 1000 kg of unprocessed, unpackaged milk. This functional unit was chosen, as milk is the main source of income on dairy farms and is, therefore, the most relevant measure for dairy farmers. Fredeen et al. (2002) and Juurlink (2004) have shown that there is no significant difference in the fat and protein content of milk produced in confinement and pasture-based dairy systems in Nova Scotia.

Therefore, no transformation of milk production values was required to account for differences in milk energy content. The allocation of material and energy flows and associated environmental burdens between milk and meat employed here reflects the biophysical relationship between the intake of food energy and the energy required for milk production, main- tenance, growth and pregnancy. Energy require- ment calculations were taken from the NRC's Nutrient Requirements of Dairy Cattle (National Research Council Subcommittee on Dairy Cattle Nutrition, 1989), as they appeared in the IPCC's Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inven- tories: Reference Manual (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996). We did not undertake systems expan- sion as a means to avoid the milk-meat allocation problem in part because there are insufficient data regarding beef production systems in Nova Scotia. Furthermore, we felt that allocating impacts based on energy requirements for milk production would provide a more direct and tangible relation- ship between feeding regime and environmental impacts, making these results more relevant to our objectives, and to local farmers and agricultural professionals.

Allocation of environmental impacts associated with the production of inputs to concentrate feeds was performed according to the relative mass of co-products. Although economic allocation is often used in the analysis of feed production, we used mass as it represents a consistent physical relationship between co-products, and, conse- quently, results will be stable through time.

16: In this phase, all inputs and outputs associated with the provision of the product throughout its life cycle are identified and quantified relative to the delivery of the functional unit of interest.
17: At this point, the complex network of environ- mental burdens identified in the inventory analysis is distilled down into fewer aggregate impact categories. In this way, it is easier to assess, under- stand and communicate the environmental

significance of the product system (Guinée, 2001b; ISO, 2006a, 2006b). In a comparative LCA, such as the one performed here, the LCIA allows the environmental impacts of the systems to be directly compared in a much more succinct and meaningful way than can be performed using just the LCI results. As is the case during the LCI phase, contribution to a particular impact category is calculated relative to the delivery of the func- tional unit.

The impact assessment method used was devel- oped at the Centre for Environmental Science (CML), at Lieden University, and has been described in detail by Guinée (2001a). To facilitate the analysis, we employed SimaPro 6.0, from Pré Consultants. The following CML impact categories were chosen:

<list>Abiotic depletion 
Global warming potential (over a 100 year time horizon) 
Ozone layer depletion 
Human toxicity 
Freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity 
Terrestrial ecotoxicity 
Photochemical oxidation 
Acidification 
Eutrophication 
Land use</list>

While not part of the CML impact assessment methodology, we chose also to calculate cumulat- ive energy demand as an additional impact cat- egory. A detailed description of the impact categories and the characterization models used appear in Arsenault (2006). It should be noted here, however, that although inputs and impacts of pesticide production are included in this analy- sis, impacts associated with their use are not. Due to insufficient data on pesticide emissions for our climatic and soil conditions, pesticide fate modeling for all of the crop systems and growing conditions was beyond the scope of the current analysis.

Normalization, an optional step during the impact assessment process, was also undertaken. This step entails the expression of LCIA results rela- tive to total global or regional contribution to impacts. In this study, we normalized the LCIA results to global contributions to impacts in 1995 (Guinée, 2001b). See Arsenault (2006: 56) for the
normalization factors used. Note that land use and cumulative energy demand values were not normali- zed, as no normalization factors exist in the CML methodology for these impact categories.

19: Interpretation is the last phase of LCA. Together, goal and scope definition and interpretations frame an LCA study, while the LCI and LCIA stages provide the necessary data and analysis to satisfy the study goals. During this stage, all decisions made throughout the study are evaluated, and conclusions are drawn based on the results of the LCI and LCIA stages (Guinee, 2001b).

Sensitivity analysis was performed to test the effects of assumptions made in this study. The details of this analysis appear later in the next section. Furthermore, in order to better interpret the implications of our results, we modelled two alternative grazing scenarios. The first explores the effects of decreasing the grazing season from five to three months while the second considers the effects of increasing the grazing season to seven months. Modelling these scenarios makes clearer the effects of using pasture as a forage source.
20: Data used to define the main product systems (the dairy farm systems) as well as many of the unit processes (e.g. the feed systems) were provided by local agricultural scientists, consultants and industry representatives. Data not available locally were obtained from LCA databases embedded in SimaPro 6.0 software (see Arsenault, 2006: 57). Whenever possible, data representing current (within the last five years) average technol- logies or conditions were used. Methods for calculating greenhouse gas and nutrient emissions from the product systems and the unit processes were gleaned from both Canadian and inter- national sources (Brentrup et al., 2000; Ceder- berg & Flysjö, 2004; Desjardins et al., 2001; Forage and Corn Variety Evaluation Task Group Atlantic Canada, n.d.; Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 1996; Main, 2001; Ontario Ministry of Agriculture Food and Rural Affairs, 2004).

6: <header>Abiotic depletion </header>

In both systems, the production and transport of concentrate feeds is an important driver of abiotic depletion, contributing nearly 49% in the modelled confinement system (Table 8) and a very similar 45% in the pasture system (Table 9). The provision of hay silage is also an important contributor, amounting to nearly 30% of the total impact in this category. This is explained in large part by the provision and use of nitrogen fertilizers.
7: <header>Global warming </header>

12: <header>Terrestrial, fresh water and human toxicity </header>

Electricity generation is a substantial contributor to terrestrial ecotoxicity, accounting for 45% and 47% of contributions from confinement and pasture systems respectively (Tables 8 and 9). This substantial contribution to toxicity is not surpris- ing, as approximately 75% of Nova Scotia's electri- city is produced from coal (Lipp et al., 2005), resulting in many toxic emissions (Babbitt & Lindner, 2006; Dubnov et al., 2006; Hylander & Goodsite, 2006; Lee et al., 2006; Xie et al., 2006). In contrast, transport-related emissions associated with the production and delivery of concentrate feeds are by far the largest driver of human and fresh water toxicity impacts in both systems. In the confinement system, transport contributes 57% to human toxicity and 76% to fresh water eco- toxicity (Table 8). Similarly, transport contributes

54% and 73% to human and fresh water toxicity, respectively, in the pasture system (Table 9). When interpreting our ecotoxicity results, however, it should be remembered that human and ecotoxicological impacts associated with pesti- cide use have been excluded.
13: <header>Acidification </header>

Ammonia emissions from manure storage contribute 47% and 37%, for the confinement and pasture systems, respectively, to the acidification potential of the modelled dairy systems (Tables 8 and 9). Also important, forage production, especially corn silage, contributes 28% to acidification potential in the confinement system (Table 8) and 35% in the pasture system (Table 9).
14: <header>Eutrophication </header>

The production of forages is responsible for 49% of the potential eutrophying emissions from the con- finement system and 59% from the pasture system. Manure storage is another major source of potential eutrophying emissions, contributing 34% and 24% to the confinement and pasture systems, respectively (Tables 8 and 9).
15: Emissions directly from dairy farms (methane from enteric fermentation and stored manure, and nitrous oxide and ammonia from manure) contrib- ute substantially to potential photochemical oxi- dation. These emissions are responsible for just over 50% of total smog generating emissions from both confinement and pasture systems (Tables 8 and 9). An additional approximately 30% of smog producing emissions from both systems comes from the provision of concentrate feeds (Tables 8 and 9).
16: Concentrate feed provision also makes substantial contributions to ozone layer depletion, amounting to about 80% of total ozone-depleting emissions from both systems (Tables 8 and 9). This contri- bution is almost entirely attributed to transport- related emissions. Corn silage is the second most important contributor, accounting for 15% of the total impact from both systems (Table 8 and 9).
17: Again, the provision of concentrate feeds play a dominant role in the overall energy required to produce milk in Nova Scotia accounting for over 50% of the cumulative energy demand in both

systems. Of that, nearly 20% results from the energy inputs to transport (Tables 8 and 9). On-farm forage production contributes just over 30% of total energy demand, with hay/haylage production predominating in both systems.

3: Not surprisingly, the production and delivery of concentrate and forage feeds account for essentially 100% of land used to produce milk. In the confine- ment system, concentrate and forage feeds contrib- ute 37% and 63%, respectively, and in the pasture system, these feeds account for 23% and 77%, respectively (Tables 8 and 9).
4: It is clear that the production and transport of con- centrate feeds drive many of the impacts considered in this study. Moreover, the provision of forages

and especially silages, also make substantial contri- butions across the board. In the pasture system, it is important to note that corn silage and hay silage are much more important contributors to impacts than is the provision of pasture (Table 9), in large part because pasture systems do not require as much chemical fertilization. The dominant role that feed subsystems play in the production of milk in Nova Scotia requires further exposition. Taking corn and pasturage as two broadly representative feed subsystems, the sources of most impacts are the pro- vision and use of nitrogen fertilizers and fossil fuels for motive power on farms (Figures 2 and 3).
Stepping back and considering the impacts associated with the production of 1000 kg of milk from pasture- and confinement-based farms, it is interesting that the two systems made similar
Table 8 Detailed contributions (% of total) of subprocesses to environmental impacts - confinement-based farm (FU = 1000 kg milk at farm gate)
Table 9 Detailed contribution (% of total) of subprocesses to environmental impacts - pasture-based farm (FU = 1000 kg milk at farm gate)

contributions to all impact categories analysed with the largest difference amounting to just over 12% in the case of acidification (Figure 4). Confinement- based milk production results in marginally greater contributions to: ozone layer depletion, human toxicity, fresh water ecotoxicity, photoche- mical oxidation, acidification and eutrophication (Figure 4). In contrast, the pasture system results in a slightly larger potential contribution to abiotic resource depletion and land use. The two systems contribute almost identically to global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and cumulative energy demand (Figure 4). Given the current inter- est in greenhouse gas emissions of food systems, it is interesting to note that typical Nova Scotia milk production results in emissions of just under one tonne CO2 equivalent per tonne raw milk at the farm gate (Tables 8 and 9).

15: As a result of the wide variety of management schemes employed by dairy farmers in Nova Scotia, it was difficult to characterize 'typical' Nova Scotian confinement-based and pasture-based systems. We feel confident, however, that the assumptions made here are reasonable and sound, as they were based on extensive consultation with local agricultural scientists. That said, it is import- ant to realize that no dairy farm in Nova Scotia is exactly like the systems modelled here.

One of the more contentious assumptions made in this study is that pasture-based systems produce less milk than confinement-based systems during the hottest period of the grazing season. Nova Scotia is thought to have a climate that is well-suited to pasture production (ample
Figure 2 Major contributors to impacts associated with corn production in Canada

precipitation during the spring and adequate pre- cipitation throughout the summer and early autumn) (Papadopoulos et al., 2005), and research has shown that, with good management aimed at the maintenance of high-quality herbage, pasture-

based farms can achieve at least 9000 kg milk per year per cow, with fewer grain inputs (Fredeen et al., 2002; Juurlink, 2004). Despite this potential, however, use of pasture in the dairy sector is declining (ADLIC, 2005), partly
Figure 3 Major contributors to impacts associated with the provision of pasture in Nova Scotia

Figure 4 A comparison of the LCIA results for confinement-based and pasture-based dairy farms in Nova Scotia. For each impact category, the system with the greatest impact is set at 100% and the contribution of the other system is shown relative to 100%

because confinement, and higher supplementation rates can increase milk production and revenue. Furthermore, hot and dry periods reduce pasture qua- lity. Consequently, milk production was assumed to be 20% less during the grazing period (a 5% decrease over the year).

To test the effect of this assumption, we undertook a sensitivity analysis in which the pasture system was remodelled, this time without the assumed decrease in milk output during the grazing season. The results indicate that relative to the base-case pasture system analysed, contributions to all impacts are reduced by 5% and the pasture-based system would potentially perform better than the confinement-based system in every category except land use (Figure 6).

18: Reducing the grazing period from five months (base case) to 3 months, generally resulted in slightly greater impacts than the base case pasture system. In particular, contribution to fresh water ecotoxi- city increased by 7% (Figure 7). Generally, the 3-month grazing scenario performed similarly to
the confinement-based system, but did result in an 8% higher abiotic depletion potential and a 6% smaller contribution related to acidification and cumulative energy demand (Figure 7).

In contrast, increasing grazing from 5 to 7 months generally resulted in marginally smaller potential impacts than the base case pasture system modelled. The maximum improvement was a 7% decrease in acidification potential (Figure 7). The most marked differences, however, occur between the seven month grazing scenario and the modelled confinement-based system. The extended grazing scenario performed better in seven of the 11 impact categories when compared to the confinement system with reductions of between 10% and 20% in the case of acidification potential, ozone depletion potential, human toxicity and fresh water ecotoxi- city (Figure 7). Perhaps not surprisingly, land use requirements were markedly higher with extended grazing (Figure 7). Interestingly, the two base case systems and two alternative grazing scenarios per- formed very similarly with respect to global warming potential as these impacts are driven largely by nonfeed related inputs (Tables 8 and 9).

Interestingly, the two base case systems and two alternative grazing scenarios per- formed very similarly with respect to global warming potential as these impacts are driven largely by nonfeed related inputs (Tables 8 and 9).

20: Reducing the grazing period from five months (base case) to 3 months, generally resulted in slightly greater impacts than the base case pasture system. the confinement-based system, but did result in an 8% higher abiotic depletion potential and a 6% smaller contribution related to acidification and cumulative energy demand (Figure 7).

In contrast, increasing grazing from 5 to 7 months generally resulted in marginally smaller potential impacts than the base case pasture system modelled. The maximum improvement was a 7% decrease in acidification potential (Figure 7). The most marked differences, however, occur between the seven month grazing scenario and the modelled confinement-based system. 21: Within the last 20 years, the number of dairy farms in Nova Scotia declined by 40% from 686 to 274 (AAFC, 2005) and the number of COWS declined from 33,000 to 24,600 over this period. The result has been an overall increase in average dairy herd size from approximately 48 COWS per farm in
1990 to approximately 71 COWS per farm in 2004. This increase in herd size has driven the shift in man- agement system from opportunistic use of pasture, to total confinement.
Contrary to our expectations at the outset of our study, the confinement- and pasture-based systems performed similarly with respect to the impact categories chosen. However, when one appreciates
Figure 6 Life cycle impact assessment results of sensitivity analysis to test the effect of not reducing milk yield by 5% in the pasture system

the impacts associated with concentrate feed pro- vision and the substantial inputs of concentrates to both systems (Tables 5 and 6), the similar impacts are not SO surprising. Both systems are high-producing and rely heavily on the supplemen- tation of grains to maximize milk production. While pasture-based farms reduce their concentrate consumption over the grazing season (recall that the pasture system modelled here was characterized by a 20% reduction in concentrates fed during the five- month grazing period), this does not constitute a large reduction in total annual concentrate feed use (less than a 5% reduction over the year). Fur- thermore, a decrease in milk production is also assumed during the grazing season, which means that the effects of reducing concentrate feed inputs are somewhat mitigated by a similar decrease in production.

The important role that the provision of forage and concentrate feeds make to most impact cat- egories considered (all save photochemical oxidation potential) is in broad agreement with prior published dairy LCAs, which identify the production and

transport of feeds as a major driver of impacts in all categories (Berlin, 2002; Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003). A unique feature of our results is the non-trivial contribution that electricity production makes to most impact categories, and in particular terrestrial ecotoxicity, largely as a result of Nova Scotia's heavy reliance on coal for the pro- duction of electricity. Other important hotspots are emissions of methane from enteric fermentation, which contribute to global warming potential, and of ammonia from manure, which contributes to photochemical oxidation and acidification potential. These on-farm emissions have also been noted as substantial contributors to impacts in other dairy LCAs (Berlin, 2002; Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003).

Although the systems perform quite similarly with respect to cumulative energy demand, the seven- month grazing scenario resulted in lower contri- butions to many impact categories while maintaining the same energy efficiency as the confinement-based
Figure 7 Life cycle impact assessment results of alternative scenarios modelled
system (Figure 7). This is interesting as some have argued that the move to confinement results in greater energy efficiency.

It is apparent, then, that reducing fossil energy consumption to transport and electricity, as well as formulating cows' diets carefully to deliver required energy and nutrients with as little impact as possible, are advisable strategies for reducing environmental impact - an insight previously identified by Hospido et al. (2003) in their analysis of Spanish milk production. For example, studies have shown that feed additives such as fish oil (Fievez et al.,

2003), probiotics and plant extracts (Boadi et al., 2004), among others, can reduce the emission of methane from enteric fermentation. Moreover, careful diet formulation will help reduce nitrogen- ous emissions from manure (Desjardins et al., 2001; Frank & Swensson, 2002; Frank et al., 2002; James et al., 1999). Although we have not modelled the potential contributions to environ- mental impacts from these alternative feed inputs, they have the potential to contribute to a net reduction in greenhouse gas, eutrophying, acidify- ing and smog producing emissions.

More generally, additional research is needed to determine feed formulations that optimize both nutritional quality and environmental performance. While it was beyond the scope of this analysis to for- mulate a minimal impact alternative ration, we can make the following observations. Inputs of chemi- cal fertilizers, the transport of inputs to concentrate feeds and their delivery to farms make substantial contributions to almost every impact category regardless of the farm system modelled (Tables 8 and 9). Consequently, a reduction in imported con- centrate inputs together with increased use of on-farm feed resources will translate into potential improvements in environmental performance. It is for this reason that the seven month grazing scen- ario (Figure 7) resulted in lower environmental impact in seven of the 11 impact categories mod- elled. Furthermore, the provision of pasture contributes less to all impacts modelled in this study than does the provision of stored forages. This suggests that a lower input farm that optimizes use of on-farm pasturelands, thereby reducing the demand for concentrates and stored silages, would show better environmental perform- ance, with similar or slightly reduced cumulative energy demand, than the two typical systems mod- elled here. Dairy LCAs from Sweden and Germany have also identified this opportunity for improvement (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001).

Some dairy systems in Nova Scotia do make more intensive use of pasture than we have mod- elled and, therefore, rely less heavily on concentrate feeds during the grazing season. One farmer who was visited in the process of gathering data for this study has shown that it is possible to feed a diet composed 85% of forages (65% pasture and 20% silage) during the grazing season, while main- taining milk production of about 26 kg/cow/day during this period. This type of farm exemplifies a management intensive grazing feeding regime that is currently not common among Nova Scotian pasture systems. However, as pasture in Nova Scotia can potentially be employed for longer, and can compose a larger percent of lactating cows' diets, and, as our results suggest, there are likely to be nontrivial benefits of doing so, it would be interesting to model more management intensive pasture systems, such as the one described above. Ideally such an analysis would extend to

considerations of animal health and on-farm biodi- versity. Anecdotal and observational evidence from Nova Scotia and elsewhere suggests that increased use of good quality pasture could result in improve- ments in both of these domains (see for e.g. Haas, 2001).

As nitrogen fertilizers are important sources of environmental impacts, dairy farmers can help to reduce environmental burdens by carefully control- ling and reducing on-farm fertilizer use in forage production. One way to do SO is to incorporate a larger proportion of nitrogen-fixing legumes into the forage mix. Finally, reducing fossil fuel energy use must be a priority for farmers. Options include using biomass, wind or solar energy, or exploring the use of manure wastes as a source of fuel gas for electricity production, as is being tested successfully on some dairy farms (Dvorak, 2005; Lincoln & Carlson, 2003), thereby reducing both direct greenhouse gas emissions and reliance on externally sourced electricity.

Some dairy systems in Nova Scotia do make more intensive use of pasture than we have mod- elled and, therefore, rely less heavily on concentrate feeds during the grazing season. One farmer who was visited in the process of gathering data for this study has shown that it is possible to feed a diet composed 85% of forages (65% pasture and 20% silage) during the grazing season, while main- taining milk production of about 26 kg/cow/day during this period. This type of farm exemplifies a management intensive grazing feeding regime that is currently not common among Nova Scotian pasture systems. However, as pasture in Nova Scotia can potentially be employed for longer, and can compose a larger percent of lactating cows' diets, and, as our results suggest, there are likely to be nontrivial benefits of doing so, it would be interesting to model more management intensive pasture systems, such as the one described above. Ideally such an analysis would extend to

considerations of animal health and on-farm biodi- versity. Anecdotal and observational evidence from Nova Scotia and elsewhere suggests that increased use of good quality pasture could result in improve- ments in both of these domains (see for e.g. Haas, 2001).

As nitrogen fertilizers are important sources of environmental impacts, dairy farmers can help to reduce environmental burdens by carefully control- ling and reducing on-farm fertilizer use in forage production. One way to do SO is to incorporate a larger proportion of nitrogen-fixing legumes into the forage mix. Finally, reducing fossil fuel energy use must be a priority for farmers. Options include using biomass, wind or solar energy, or exploring the use of manure wastes as a source of fuel gas for electricity production, as is being tested successfully on some dairy farms (Dvorak, 2005; Lincoln & Carlson, 2003), thereby reducing both direct greenhouse gas emissions and reliance on externally sourced electricity.

1990 to approximately 71 COWS per farm in 2004. This increase in herd size has driven the shift in man- agement system from opportunistic use of pasture, to total confinement.
Contrary to our expectations at the outset of our study, the confinement- and pasture-based systems performed similarly with respect to the impact categories chosen. However, when one appreciates
Figure 6 Life cycle impact assessment results of sensitivity analysis to test the effect of not reducing milk yield by 5% in the pasture system

the impacts associated with concentrate feed pro- vision and the substantial inputs of concentrates to both systems (Tables 5 and 6), the similar impacts are not SO surprising. Both systems are high-producing and rely heavily on the supplemen- tation of grains to maximize milk production. While pasture-based farms reduce their concentrate consumption over the grazing season (recall that the pasture system modelled here was characterized by a 20% reduction in concentrates fed during the five- month grazing period), this does not constitute a large reduction in total annual concentrate feed use (less than a 5% reduction over the year). Fur- thermore, a decrease in milk production is also assumed during the grazing season, which means that the effects of reducing concentrate feed inputs are somewhat mitigated by a similar decrease in production.

transport of feeds as a major driver of impacts in all categories (Berlin, 2002; Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000; Haas et al., 2001; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003). A unique feature of our results is the non-trivial contribution that electricity production makes to most impact categories, and in particular terrestrial ecotoxicity, largely as a result of Nova Scotia's heavy reliance on coal for the pro- duction of electricity. Although the systems perform quite similarly with respect to cumulative energy demand, the seven- month grazing scenario resulted in lower contri- butions to many impact categories while maintaining the same energy efficiency as the confinement-based
Figure 7 Life cycle impact assessment results of alternative scenarios modelled
system (Figure 7). This is interesting as some have argued that the move to confinement results in greater energy efficiency.

29: The current trend in the dairy industry in Nova Scotia, and across Canada, towards fewer, larger, confinement-based farms is not occurring without potential environmental impacts. These farms, characterized by relatively high production levels, are often perceived by some to be more efficient and therefore more environmentally sustainable than other systems, including those employing pasture. However, given the impacts associated with keeping livestock in high concentrations and with increased grain-feed supplementation, and considering the potential environmental and quality of life advantages associated with pasture- based dairy management, there is growing concern that this trend towards the industrialization of Canada's dairy farms is not sustainable.

Life cycle assessment is a useful platform from which to evaluate environmental impacts of alterna- tive milk production systems. An inventory of all inputs to and emissions from typical contemporary pasture- and confinement-based dairy farm systems in Nova Scotia was constructed from existing data. These data were used to assess a wide range of impacts associated with producing 1000 kg of milk from these systems. While this approach does not
provide an exhaustive basis to assess the sustainabil- ity of dairy systems, it does provide a very robust assessment of the environmental impacts of dairy industry strategies.

Somewhat surprisingly, our results indicate that the two modelled systems perform quite similarly with respect to the impact categories studied. This is explained mainly by the high use of concentrate feeds in both systems. Further scenario modelling indicates that a greater reliance on pasture, thereby decreasing inputs of concentrates and stored silages, could improve environmental per- formance of dairying, while maintaining compar- able life cycle energy efficiency. Given these results, the trend in the Canadian industry towards a greater number of confinement-based units is a concern as it is occurring without sufficient regard for environmental impacts. In Nova Scotia, the transition to full confinement does not result in environmental benefits. Indeed, some of the mod- elled impacts were higher in the confinement system. More research, using systems-based bio- physical accounting tools such as LCA, is needed to account fully for the effects of the trend towards increased industrialization in Canadian dairy farming and to provide a detailed assessment of the relative merits of alternative management systems. For example, further work is needed to determine the concentrate and forage feeding regimes which minimize environmental impacts.
